response.description_long:
Every engineering team above fifty people has built a feature-flag system by accident: a config table, a cache, a Slack channel where someone asks "is it safe to delete this row?" Shipglass is a feature management platform designed to replace that accidental complexity with something deliberate. Founded in Denver in 2021 by Casey Morrow and Ben Alvarez — who previously ran release engineering where a bad Friday deploy meant a weekend — the company addresses the gap between deploy and release, making the latter a runtime decision rather than a code push.

The product works by wrapping any change in a flag, rolling it out to a percentage of users, watching metrics, and ramping or rolling back without a deploy. SDKs for 12 languages — JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, Java, Kotlin, Swift, Ruby, PHP, C#, Rust, and Elixir — evaluate flags locally from a streamed ruleset, adding well under 1 millisecond and working offline. An edge network with points of presence on every populated continent keeps rulesets within 50 milliseconds of users worldwide, so a flag change published in the dashboard reaches every connected SDK in under a second globally. If the stream drops, the SDK serves the last ruleset it saw and reconciles on reconnect; a brand-new process falls back to bundled defaults.

Targeting rules combine user attributes, cohorts, and percentage rollouts. Percentage bucketing is deterministic per user, so ramps are stable across sessions and services. Cohorts can sync from a data warehouse or build from attributes; entitlement flags can mirror billing plans so plan gating lives in one place. Every flag change is audited — who, what, when, from which environment — in an immutable audit log streamable to a SIEM. Approvals can be required per environment. Stale flags are surfaced automatically after being fully ramped for 30 days, evaluated but never varying, or referenced in code but never evaluated, with a cleanup pull request generated for supported languages.

Any rollout can become an A/B test: attach a metric, and Shipglass computes lift with confidence intervals. Metrics come from your event stream or warehouse. Guardrail metrics watch error rates and latency during every ramp, and a ramp that degrades a guardrail pauses itself and pages the flag owner.

The company targets product engineering teams that ship daily. Common first uses include risky migrations, tiered entitlements, and gradual mobile rollouts where app-store review makes rollback expensive. The migration pattern: wrap the new database path in a flag, ramp reads to 1 percent, compare results in the shadow, ramp to 100, then delete the old path. The entitlement pattern: model plan tiers as flags so packaging changes ship without a deploy. The mobile pattern: ship the feature dark in the binary, have app review happen once, and ramp server-side over days — rollback is a toggle, not a resubmission.

Pricing starts with a Free tier: up to 5 seats, unlimited flags, all SDKs, one production environment. Pro is $12 per seat per month, adding unlimited environments, approvals, experiments, stale-flag cleanup, and an audit log. Enterprise adds SAML, custom roles, and a dedicated environment with annual billing. Fully self-hosted is on the roadmap but not close — the edge network is most of the product.

The company's team of 27 previously built release tooling at large consumer-software companies. Shipglass's engineering blog — particularly a piece titled "The config table and the prayer" that autopsies homegrown flag systems — circulates widely among platform teams. The founders regard per-flag pricing as a tax on the discipline flags require, hence the free tier's unlimited flags. Shipglass publishes its edge network's ruleset delivery latency openly on a status page.
